author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
===========================
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

@everyone   

Please confirm you have read this with a 🤑

Hey <@1261035908765843508>,
Huge congrats — after 10 months of real work (learning, paper, backtesting, facing doubts head-on), you're stepping up and going live today. That's not just a start; that's a massive milestone.

You're nervous? Totally normal — it means you care and you're taking it seriously. You've built the foundation with patience and practice; now trust it, stick to your rules, size small, and let the process guide you.

Luck, focus, and calm hands today.

author: Lianne
content: Thank you for the kind words <@1014389514141184074> ! I thought I did a really good trade, to find out I didn't take the trade lol. I was using IBKR for the first time live and got some popups and wanted to enter as fast as I could, but somehow I apparently didn't enter. Which I found out after I was done and couldn't find my trade. So that was kind of stupid 😅  But because I was thinking I was in a live trade, I experienced everything. The nerves were killing me. I felt sick, I literally put my hands under my legs and sit on them, not selling too soon and sticking to my plan. Although it would have been nice if I had such a successful trade, more important was I stuck to my plan, even though in my head I was screaming: SELL!!!!. So, good day. Satisfied. Gonna do it all tomorrow again, now better paying attention if I actually enter or not lol.

author: Hugo Comte
content:
Hi everyone.

Transitionning from stocks to options is tricky atm  as i have issues with contracts/sizing and id really like some help to understand how to size properly and pick the right contracts, as im not able to TP correctly atm.

Todays trade was a long on AMZN.
I got in at 242.8 with 4 contracts of the 242.5

with the risk reward simulator of Trading view, 400 shares ( 4 contracts ) is  a 300$ risk on my stop stop loss ,
However at 1R i was only in profit of 190$ ( just 0.65R ) 

Why is that ?
Should i have taken the 245 contracts ? the move should have been 3R in order to be in the money.
or buy more contract ? but why doesn't it correlates  with the defined risk in $

author: Luke
content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

author: Huy
content: I must say i did AMAZING today. I was live trading with Jack. I had my contracts for MU pulled up but something didn't feel right for whatever reason so I calmly watched MU took off from us. Then I avoided the NVDA trade which didn't work out for a lot of people. That play personally did not resonate with me so I skipped. Mind you i did make .5 % off my portfolio after Powell had his speech but this was much later in on the day. I was okay with not trading at all but saw a good opportunity and jumped on it. One amazing way to stay disciplined is to just let trades go if you didn't get in on time. Trading is not like Pokemon so we simply can't catch them all!

author: Bhargav
content:
I started trading 1 year and 10 months ago. Had gone through some bad months. I won't say it was easy. It was a roller coaster ride. But not giving up and improving my skills day by day, like being patient, executing only A+ trades, and understanding the market well. I saw the results. So I took a small challenge to grow a small account from $500 to $5000 a month. You guys have seen the results. But today I'm happy to share the results from the first week of Feb. I was able to grow a small account from $500 to $4000 in a week. This has been my greatest achievement so far.

author: Huy
content: Vwap / ema 9 are good “side” confluences that you can use to help to mentally stay in a trade longer but they are still lagging indicators. They form based on the price action that just occurred. So this means being able to read candles like jack and Tony is the preferred way in the long run.

author: Z33TRADES
content:
Hey everyone,

I wanted to ask if any of you have experienced issues exiting option positions on IBKR.

Last Friday, I took the same NVDA 0DTE trade as Tony and tried to cut the position when it hit my mental stop. However, my sell order wouldn’t fill. I attempted to sell five different times before it finally executed, and by then I ended up taking about a $1,000 larger loss than necessary.

Today I ran into something similar with Microsoft (4 DTE contracts). I was in profit and tried to close the position, but again had trouble getting filled. I still made money, but I could have realized more profit if the order had executed when I first tried to exit.

It’s definitely frustrating. I’m brand new to both trading and IBKR, so I’m trying to figure out whether I’m doing something wrong or if there are specific settings or execution strategies I should be aware of.

Any insight would be appreciated.

author: Huy
content:
that wasn't a reclaim of the 5 mins high. It broke past it and you entered before any retest on the 5 mins high. AMD was a tough one today. You had 3 levels near each other.
- PMH
- PDH
- 5 min high
PHD has priority in this context
note: purple line is 5 mins high; yellow line is pdh
textbook volume profile on the break and retest candles
Good setup but it just didn't workout today. Not an A+ since QQQ was below its PDH during this time though.
This entry would usually work other times

author: Jerry
content:
Depends on the risk. 90% of my trades are with the same size. If risk is higher than 3$, I decrease size. If risk is lower than 1$ I increase size. Today risk was small so I took 40 cons instead of 30 cons standard size

P.s. no I dont care about dollar amount size. Only number of cons is important
